timeline.event: Birth of Nzinga Mbande
Seized power in Ndongo after the death of her brother Mbandi
Ruled the Kingdom of Ndongo
Portugal declared war on Ndongo
Nzinga's army became exhausted, leading to her exile
Rebuilt her army through an Imbangala alliance and conquered the Kingdom of Matamba
Ruled the Kingdom of Matamba
Allied with the Dutch West India Company, who had captured Luanda from Portugal
Reclaimed most of Ndongo
Portugal retook Luanda, and the Dutch left Angola
Signed a peace treaty with Portugal
Death of Nzinga Mbande

keyAchievements.title: Resolution of political crises
keyAchievements.description: Received military and political education in her youth and demonstrated an aptitude for resolving political crises as an ambassador to the Portuguese Empire.

keyAchievements.title: Conquest of the Kingdom of Matamba
keyAchievements.description: Rebuilt her army through an Imbangala alliance and conquered the Kingdom of Matamba from 1631 to 1635.

keyAchievements.title: Recovery of Ndongo territory
keyAchievements.description: Through an alliance with the Dutch, she was able to reclaim most of Ndongo between 1641 and 1644.

keyAchievements.title: Victories against Portugal
keyAchievements.description: Allied with the Dutch to defeat Portugal in several battles.

faq.question: Which kingdoms did Nzinga Mbande rule?
faq.answer: Nzinga Mbande ruled the Ambundu Kingdoms of Ndongo (1624–1663) and Matamba (1631–1663), located in present-day northern Angola.

faq.question: When did Nzinga Mbande seize power in Ndongo?
faq.answer: Nzinga Mbande seized power over Ndongo after her brother Mbandi died in 1624.

faq.question: What alliances did Nzinga Mbande make?
faq.answer: Nzinga Mbande married the Imbangala general Kasanje to secure an alliance, and in 1641 she allied with the Dutch West India Company, who had captured Luanda from Portugal.

timeline.event: Born on the island of Samos in Asia Minor
Driven out of Mytilene after giving lectures, due to backlash
Founded 'The Garden' in Athens as the meeting place of his school
Died from kidney stones

keyAchievements.title: Founded Epicureanism
keyAchievements.description: Founded the philosophical school of Epicureanism.

keyAchievements.title: Proposed the purpose of philosophy
keyAchievements.description: Believed that the purpose of philosophy is to attain a happy and tranquil life.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of Hedonistic philosophy
keyAchievements.description: Argued for atomistic materialism and hedonism in ethics, becoming the founder of hedonistic philosophy.

keyAchievements.title: Established the concepts of Ataraxia and Aponia
keyAchievements.description: Proposed ataraxia (peace and freedom from fear) and aponia (the absence of pain) as the characteristics of a happy and tranquil life.

keyAchievements.title: Proposed a perspective on death
keyAchievements.description: Taught that death is the end of the body and soul, and therefore should not be feared.

keyAchievements.title: Proposed a perspective on gods
keyAchievements.description: Argued that gods neither punish nor reward humans and do not concern themselves with human affairs.

keyAchievements.title: Developed atomistic materialism
keyAchievements.description: Explained that all phenomena in the world result from the movements and interactions of atoms moving in empty space.

keyAchievements.title: Introduced the concept of free will
keyAchievements.description: Stated that atomic motion is not just straight lines but occasionally swerves, thereby avoiding determinism and granting free will.

keyAchievements.title: Introduced the idea of human equality
keyAchievements.description: Introduced the fundamental idea of human equality to the Greeks by accepting women and slaves into his school.

keyAchievements.title: Proposed the principle of multiple explanations
keyAchievements.description: Proposed the 'principle of multiple explanations', contributing to the philosophy of science.

faq.question: What was the core purpose of Epicurean philosophy?
faq.answer: For Epicurus, the purpose of philosophy was to attain a happy and tranquil life characterized by ataraxia (peace and freedom from fear) and aponia (absence of pain).

faq.question: What did Epicurus teach about death?
faq.answer: Epicurus taught that death is the end of the body and soul and should not be feared. He stated that when we exist, death is not, and when death exists, we do not; therefore, death is nothing to us.

faq.question: What were the characteristics of the Epicurean school?
faq.answer: The Epicurean school was the first to officially accept women. It regarded friendship as an important ingredient for happiness and supported seclusion over political participation.

faq.question: What does pleasure mean to Epicurus?
faq.answer: Epicurus viewed pleasure as the absence of pain, and pursued continuous, static pleasure rather than momentary, physical pleasure.

timeline.event: Born on September 22 as the third of four children to James Faraday and Margaret in Newington.
Became an apprentice to George Riebau, a bookseller, at the age of 13.
Signed a seven-year contract as George Riebau's apprentice on August 7.
Read Jane Marcet's 'Conversations on Chemistry' and the 3rd edition of the Encyclopædia Britannica by James Tytler.
Began taking notes on articles about art and science from newspapers and magazines.
Received tickets to Humphry Davy's chemistry lectures and attended them; wrote a letter asking Davy for help.
Started working at the Royal Institution in March; began a European tour as Davy's secretary and assistant on October 13.
Traveled to Naples, Italy, climbed Mount Vesuvius, and met Alessandro Volta in Pavia.
Returned to England on April 23; re-employed as superintendent and assistant of the apparatus and mineralogical collections at the Royal Institution's laboratory in May.
Married Sarah Barnard on June 12; published an article on electromagnetic rotation in 'Annals of Philosophy'.
Left the note 'Convert magnetism into electricity' in his laboratory notebook.
Successfully liquefied chlorine.
Discovered benzene; commissioned by the Royal Society to research the optical properties of glass used for telescopes.
Determined the molecular structure of naphthalene and created two sample crystals; established the 'Christmas Lectures for Children'.
Published a paper including practical guidance on chemistry in April; began optical glass experiments with a special furnace provided by the Royal Institution.
Announced the success of his optical glass research at the Bakerian Lecture of the Royal Society.
Began electromagnetic experiments on August 29 and discovered electromagnetic induction.
Began 'Electrochemical Decomposition' research at the end of the year and discovered Faraday's first law.
Experimented with various dissolved substances in a voltameter at the end of the year and discovered Faraday's second law.
Discovered that the charge on a charged conductor resides only on its exterior surface.
Published Volume 1 of 'Experimental Researches in Electricity'.
Successfully liquefied many substances including ammonia and carbon dioxide.
Met William Thomson at Cambridge University in June; discovered the Faraday effect on September 18, defining a physical relationship between magnetism and light.
Began experiments to investigate changes in diamagnetic materials influenced by magnets, and mentioned the concept of a magnetic field.
Granted residence in a house near Hampton Court along the River Thames in London at the request of Queen Victoria.
Published 'Experimental Researches in Chemistry and Physics'.

Submitted research results on gravity and electricity to the 'Philosophical Transactions' but was advised to withdraw them; gave a lecture on 'The Chemical History of a Candle' at the Royal Institution's Christmas Lectures.
Began suffering from memory loss symptoms; gave his final lecture at Christmas.
Conducted an experiment on whether the spectrum of sodium and other metals heated by a flame is affected by an electromagnetic field.
Passed away on August 25.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of Electromagnetic Induction
keyAchievements.description: Discovered the principles of electromagnetic induction, diamagnetism, and the laws of electrolysis, and invented an electromagnetic rotary device that formed the foundation of the electric motor.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of Benzene and Introduction of Chemical Terms
keyAchievements.description: Discovered benzene as a chemist, investigated the clathrate hydrate of chlorine, and introduced technical terms such as anode, cathode, electrode, and ion for the first time.

keyAchievements.title: Successful Liquefaction of Gases
keyAchievements.description: Successfully liquefied many substances such as chlorine, ammonia, and carbon dioxide, proving the existence of critical points and the interchangeability of states of matter.

keyAchievements.title: Research on Manufacturing Optical Glass
keyAchievements.description: Researched the optical properties of glass used in telescopes and developed a method to produce uniform glass without bubbles or striae.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of the Faraday Effect
keyAchievements.description: Established the fundamental relationship between a magnetic field and light rays, showing that a magnetic field can affect light, and discovered the phenomenon of the plane of polarization rotating in a magnetic field.

keyAchievements.title: Classification of Magnetic Materials
keyAchievements.description: Named diamagnetic materials that stop perpendicularly to a magnetic field and paramagnetic materials that stop parallel to it, discovering that most gases are diamagnetic while oxygen has paramagnetism.

keyAchievements.title: Introduction of the Magnetic Field Concept
keyAchievements.description: Believed that lines of magnetic force had physical meaning and first mentioned the concept of a magnetic field, where the influence from two magnetic poles varies depending on the position.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of the Faraday Cage Principle
keyAchievements.description: Discovered that the electrical charge on a charged conductor resides only on its exterior and exerts no influence on anything enclosed within it.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of the Christmas Lectures
keyAchievements.description: Created the 'Christmas Lectures for Children' in 1826 to popularize science, which has been passed down as a tradition of the Royal Institution in London.

faq.question: What was Michael Faraday's educational background?
faq.answer: Faraday received little formal or higher education during his childhood, learning only the basics of reading, writing, and arithmetic. He acquired his scientific knowledge primarily through self-study.

faq.question: Was Faraday proficient in mathematics?
faq.answer: Faraday lacked mathematical skills to the point that he could only do simple algebra and could not handle trigonometry, but he excelled in visual thinking and imagination. His theories were later mathematically reformulated by James Clerk Maxwell.

faq.question: Did Faraday pursue fame or wealth?
faq.answer: No. He was not interested in worldly wealth, refused to publish his lectures, and declined the presidency of the Royal Society as well as a knighthood. His humility and frugality stemmed from his religious beliefs.

faq.question: Did Faraday have children?
faq.answer: No. Faraday married Sarah Barnard, but they had no children throughout their lives. However, he created and participated in science lectures for children, contributing to the popularization of science.

faq.question: How did Faraday's religion influence his scientific research?
faq.answer: Faraday was a devout follower of Christian fundamentalism and a member of the Sandemanian congregation. His humility, frugality, and pure pursuit of academics were closely related to this religious background.

timeline.event: Born in Falaise, Duchy of Normandy
Received oaths of fealty as the successor from the Norman magnates
Succeeded to the Duchy of Normandy as his father Robert I died in Nicaea
Started to assert his authority over the duchy by suppressing rebellions
Victorious at the Battle of Val-es-Dunes with the help of King Henry I of France
Succeeded in driving out Guy of Burgundy
Married Matilda of Flanders
King Edward of England seems to have chosen William as his heir
Defended against a two-front attack on Normandy by Henry and Norman rebels
Henry and Geoffrey Martel were defeated at the Battle of Varaville
Firmly secured his hold on the Duchy of Normandy
Seized control of neighboring Maine
Secured dominion over Maine
Invaded England with a massive fleet
Defeated and killed Harold at the Battle of Hastings
Crowned King of England at Westminster Abbey in London
Made preparations for the governance of England and returned to Normandy
Suppressed the rebellion of Edwin and Morcar, and built castles such as Warwick, York, and Nottingham
Suppressed Edgar the Aetheling's rebellion attacking York
Met with three papal legates to hold a council to reform and reorganize the English Church
Suppressed the last northern rebellion and imprisoned Morcar
Attacked Scotland, defeating Malcolm, and signed the Treaty of Abernethy
Ended the war by capturing Le Mans in Maine
Suppressed the 'Revolt of the Earls'
Defeated by King Philip of France at the Battle of Dol
Faced problems with his eldest son, Robert
Unhorsed by Robert during the siege of Gerberoy
Reached a compromise with Robert, reaffirming he would inherit Normandy upon William's death
Visited Wales
Ordered the arrest of his half-brother Odo
Death of his wife, Queen Matilda
Ordered the compilation of the Domesday Book
The Domesday Book work was mostly completed and he received the Oath of Salisbury
Launched an expedition to the Vexin in France
Died at the Priory of St Gervase in Rouen

keyAchievements.title: Solidified position as Duke of Normandy
keyAchievements.description: Firmly secured the Duchy of Normandy as his own around 1060 after a long struggle over the ducal position.

keyAchievements.title: Conquest of England
keyAchievements.description: Led the Norman conquest by invading England in 1066, winning the Battle of Hastings, and being crowned King of England.

keyAchievements.title: Compilation of the Domesday Book
keyAchievements.description: Ordered the compilation of the Domesday Book in 1086, a survey record documenting all land ownership in England.

keyAchievements.title: Laid the foundation for English governance
keyAchievements.description: Constructed various castles in England, settled Norman nobles, and changed the composition of the English clergy.

keyAchievements.title: Alliance with Flanders
keyAchievements.description: Gained a powerful ally in the neighboring County of Flanders through his marriage to Matilda of Flanders.

faq.question: When was William I born?
faq.answer: William I was born on November 8, 1028, in Falaise, Duchy of Normandy.

faq.question: How did William I become the King of England?
faq.answer: Following the death of Edward the Confessor in 1066, he invaded England, won the Battle of Hastings, and was crowned King of England in London on Christmas Day of that year.

faq.question: What is the Domesday Book?
faq.answer: The Domesday Book is a survey record ordered by William I in 1086 that documented the current status of all land ownership in England.

faq.question: When did William I die?
faq.answer: William I died on September 9, 1087, at the Priory of St Gervase in Rouen, while fighting a war in northern France.

faq.question: What was William I's family background like?
faq.answer: He was born as the illegitimate son of Robert I, Duke of Normandy, and his mistress Herleva. He married Matilda of Flanders, with whom he had four sons and either five or six daughters.

timeline.event: Born as the only son of Cambyses I, King of Anshan of Persian descent, and Mandane, daughter of King Astyages of Media
Rebelled against his maternal grandfather Astyages, starting a war with Media
Conquered the Median Empire by capturing Ecbatana
Marched to Sardis, the capital of Lydia
Defeated King Croesus of Lydia and captured Sardis
The Greek Ionian cities on the Aegean coast submitted to Persia
Invaded the Neo-Babylonian Empire and captured Babylon
Killed in action during a battle with Central Asian nomads east of the Caspian Sea

keyAchievements.title: Construction of the great Persian Empire
keyAchievements.description: Grew Persia into a vast empire encompassing most of Southwest and Central Asia and reaching India.

keyAchievements.title: Conquest of the Median Empire
keyAchievements.description: Conquered the Median Empire by capturing Ecbatana in 549 BC.

keyAchievements.title: Conquest of Lydia
keyAchievements.description: Conquered Lydia by capturing its capital Sardis in 546 BC.

keyAchievements.title: Conquest of Neo-Babylonia
keyAchievements.description: Invaded Neo-Babylonia and captured Babylon in 539 BC.

keyAchievements.title: Liberation of Jews and permission to rebuild the Temple
keyAchievements.description: Liberated the Jews held captive in Babylon, allowing them to return to Jerusalem and rebuild their sacred Temple in Jerusalem.

keyAchievements.title: Policy of religious tolerance
keyAchievements.description: Advocated a policy of religious tolerance and inclusion to harmonize the multi-ethnic Persian Empire.

keyAchievements.title: Cyrus Cylinder
keyAchievements.description: Left behind the Cyrus Cylinder, known as the first declaration of human rights that banned slavery.

faq.question: Where does the name Cyrus originate?
faq.answer: Ancient Greek historians recorded that the name Cyrus originated from 'Kuros' meaning sun, and it is related to the meaning 'like the sun' in Old Persian.

faq.question: How is Cyrus mentioned in the Bible?
faq.answer: He is recorded as 'Koresh' in the Old Testament, and appears as the king who liberated the Jews from the Babylonian captivity and allowed them to rebuild the Temple in Jerusalem.

faq.question: How did Cyrus die?
faq.answer: According to ancient historians, he was killed in a battle with Central Asian nomads east of the Caspian Sea. Herodotus recorded that he died in a battle with the queen of the Massagetae.

faq.question: What is the Cyrus Cylinder?
faq.answer: It is known as the first declaration of human rights that banned slavery, and is currently housed in Room 52 of the British Museum.

faq.question: Where is the tomb of Cyrus?
faq.answer: It is located in Pasargadae and is registered as a UNESCO World Heritage site.

timeline.event: Born in Vienna, Austria.
Obtained a Ph.D. from the University of Vienna.
Married Annemarie (Anny) Bertel.
Moved to the University of Zurich.
Published the paper 'Quantization as an Eigenvalue Problem' containing the Schrödinger equation.
Succeeded Max Planck at Friedrich Wilhelm University in Berlin.
Left Germany in opposition to Nazism.
Awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics along with Paul Dirac.
Proposed the Schrödinger's cat thought experiment.
Fired in Graz after the Austrian annexation, fled to Italy, and later relocated to Dublin.
Became the Director of the School for Theoretical Physics at the Dublin Institute for Advanced Studies.
Authored 'What Is Life?'.
Became a naturalized Irish citizen.
Retired in Dublin.
Returned to Vienna.
Died of tuberculosis in Vienna at the age of 73.
The Department of Physics at Trinity College Dublin recommended removing his name from a lecture theatre considering his history of sexual abuse.

keyAchievements.title: Development of the Schrödinger equation
keyAchievements.description: Developed an equation providing a way to calculate a system's wave function and how it changes dynamically over time, thereby developing the fundamental results of quantum mechanics.

keyAchievements.title: Nobel Prize in Physics
keyAchievements.description: Awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1933 along with Paul Dirac for the formulation of the Schrödinger equation.

keyAchievements.title: Authored 'What Is Life?'
keyAchievements.description: Viewed life phenomena from the perspective of physics and addressed the problem of genetics, inspiring the discovery of the DNA double helix structure.

keyAchievements.title: Proposed Schrödinger's cat thought experiment
keyAchievements.description: A famous thought experiment designed to mock the Copenhagen interpretation of quantum mechanics, and one of his most enduring legacies.

keyAchievements.title: Research on unified field theory
keyAchievements.description: Made several attempts to construct a unified field theory integrating gravity, electromagnetism, and nuclear forces.

keyAchievements.title: Research on color theory
keyAchievements.description: Showed a strong interest in psychology, particularly color perception and colorimetry, and published a series of papers in this field.

faq.question: For what achievement did Erwin Schrödinger win the Nobel Prize?
faq.answer: He was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1933 along with Paul Dirac for the formulation of the Schrödinger equation.

faq.question: What is Schrödinger's cat?
faq.answer: It is a famous thought experiment designed to mock the Copenhagen interpretation of quantum mechanics.

faq.question: What books did Schrödinger author?
faq.answer: He authored 'What Is Life?', which includes discussions on the genetic code of living organisms and negentropy.

faq.question: When and where was Schrödinger born and when did he die?
faq.answer: He was born in Vienna, Austria on August 12, 1887, and died of tuberculosis in Vienna on January 4, 1961, at the age of 73.

faq.question: Why did Schrödinger leave Germany?
faq.answer: He left Germany in 1933 because he despised the anti-Semitism of the Nazis.

timeline.event: Giuseppe Maria Garibaldi, an Italian general and patriot, is born.
Certified as a merchant navy captain.
Met Giovanni Battista Cuneo, an Italian political immigrant, in Taganrog, Russia, and joined the Young Italy movement.
Sentenced to death after participating in the Piedmont rebellion and fled to Marseille.
Joined the Ragamuffin rebels in Brazil and aided the independence of the Riograndense Republic.
Anita Ribeiro joined Garibaldi's ship 'Rio Pardo'.
First son Menotti is born.
Moved to Montevideo and worked as a trader and teacher.
Married Anita in Montevideo; commanded the Uruguayan fleet and raised the 'Italian Legion'.
Second daughter Rosita is born; formed the Italian Legion in Montevideo.
Third daughter Teresita is born.
Achieved victory at the Battles of Cerro and San Antonio.
Returned to his homeland Italy upon hearing news of the revolution breaking out in Palermo.
Promoted to Marshal of the Roman Republic army; fled to northern Italy after the fall of the Roman Republic; his wife Anita died during the retreat.
Became a resident of New York.
Sailed to the mouth of the River Tyne in North East England as the master of the sailing ship Commonwealth.
Returned to Italy.
Outbreak of the Second Italian War of Independence; led the 'Hunters of the Alps' and captured Varese and Como.
Landed in Marsala, Sicily leading a thousand volunteers.
Defeated the Bourbon French garrison at Calatafimi.
Launched a siege on Palermo.
Entered the capital of Naples.
The volunteers achieved success at the Battle of the Volturno.
Met and shook hands with Victor Emmanuel II in Teano.
The unified Kingdom of Italy was established.
Left Genoa, landed in Palermo, and gathered volunteers under the slogan 'Rome or Death'.
Landed at Melito and marched into the Calabrian mountains.
Met regular forces at the Aspromonte mountains and was taken prisoner after a battle.
With the backing of the Italian government, recruited the 'Hunters of the Alps', led them into Trentino, and defeated the Austrian army at Bezzecca.
Marched on Rome again but was stopped by the Papal army supported by French reinforcements.
The Franco-Prussian War broke out.
Died on the island of Caprera.

keyAchievements.title: Contribution to Italian unification
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to the unification of Italy and the establishment of the Kingdom of Italy.

keyAchievements.title: Hero of Two Worlds
keyAchievements.description: Known as the 'Hero of Two Worlds' for his military achievements across South America and Europe.

keyAchievements.title: Support for South American independence
keyAchievements.description: Joined the Ragamuffin rebels in Brazil to help the independence of the Riograndense Republic, and participated in the Uruguayan Civil War, raising the Redshirts.

keyAchievements.title: Italian military command
keyAchievements.description: Appointed general by the provisional government of Milan and promoted to Marshal of the Roman Republic army.

keyAchievements.title: Commanding the Hunters of the Alps
keyAchievements.description: Led the 'Hunters of the Alps' in the Second Italian War of Independence, capturing Varese and Como.

keyAchievements.title: Success of the Expedition of the Thousand
keyAchievements.description: Led an expedition of a thousand volunteers to successfully annex Sicily, Southern Italy, Marche, and Umbria to the Kingdom of Sardinia.

keyAchievements.title: Participation in the Franco-Prussian War
keyAchievements.description: Served as commander of the Army of the Vosges during the Franco-Prussian War.

keyAchievements.title: International symbol
keyAchievements.description: Became an international symbol for realizing the ideals of national independence and republicanism.

keyAchievements.title: Efforts to limit papal power
keyAchievements.description: Contributed to anti-clericalism among Latin liberals and did much to limit the temporal power of the Pope.

faq.question: Who was Giuseppe Garibaldi?
faq.answer: An Italian general and patriot who contributed to the unification of Italy and the establishment of the Kingdom of Italy.

faq.question: Why is Garibaldi called the 'Hero of Two Worlds'?
faq.answer: Because his military achievements span across South America and Europe.

faq.question: What were Garibaldi's political beliefs?
faq.answer: He was a republican who dedicated himself to unifying Italy under a democratic republican government.

faq.question: What is Garibaldi's iconic attire?
faq.answer: It is related to the red shirts worn by his volunteers instead of uniforms.

faq.question: What activities did Garibaldi undertake in South America?
faq.answer: He joined the Ragamuffin rebels in Brazil to aid the independence of the Riograndense Republic, and participated in the Uruguayan Civil War, raising the Redshirts.

faq.question: Did Garibaldi have a chance to participate in the American Civil War?
faq.answer: He was offered a position as a major general in the Union Army by President Abraham Lincoln, but the offer fell through when he proposed the abolition of slavery and the position of Commander-in-Chief as conditions.

faq.question: Where is Garibaldi's hometown?
faq.answer: He was born in Nice (Nizza), currently in France, while it was under French rule in 1792.

faq.question: With whom did Garibaldi have a conflict after Italian unification?
faq.answer: He deeply disliked the Sardinian Prime Minister Camillo di Cavour, owing to personal resentment over Cavour's pragmatism and the selling of Nice to the French.

timeline.event: Born in Edinburgh, Scotland
Mother died (age 8)
Produced his first scientific work, a paper on 'Oval Curves', at age 14
Enrolled at the University of Edinburgh
Began studying at the University of Cambridge
Graduated from Cambridge as Second Wrangler in the Mathematical Tripos and first in the Smith's Prize exam
Elected as a Fellow of Trinity College to supervise experiments on electricity and magnetism and research color theory
Published a paper on the structure of Saturn's rings
Married Katherine Mary Dewar
Awarded the Adams Prize for his research on Saturn's rings
Appointed Professor of Natural Philosophy and Astronomy at King's College London, and received the Rumford Medal from the Royal Society of London for his research on color vision
Produced the first color photograph by applying the mixing of the three primary colors
Published 'A Dynamical Theory of the Electromagnetic Field'
Resigned from his professorship at King's College and became a professor at Cambridge University
Statistically formulated the kinetic theory of gases
Researched at the Cavendish Laboratory
Published 'A Treatise on Electricity and Magnetism'
Began editing Henry Cavendish's unpublished electrical experiment data
Died of stomach cancer in Cambridge at the age of 48 on November 5

keyAchievements.title: Electromagnetism
keyAchievements.description: Laid a unified foundation for electrical and magnetic phenomena, established electromagnetism, and proved that light is an electromagnetic wave.

keyAchievements.title: Maxwell's equations
keyAchievements.description: Mathematically organized existing electromagnetic theories and established Maxwell's equations, the differential equations that form the basis of electromagnetism.

keyAchievements.title: Kinetic theory of gases
keyAchievements.description: Formulated the velocity distribution law of gas molecules and introduced probabilistic concepts, laying the foundation for statistical mechanics.

keyAchievements.title: Color theory and color photography
keyAchievements.description: Produced the first color photograph in 1861 by applying the principle that all colors can be expressed by mixing the three primary colors.

keyAchievements.title: Research on Saturn's rings
keyAchievements.description: Showed that Saturn's rings must be composed of small particles to be stable, a prediction proven by the Voyager spacecraft in the 1980s.

faq.question: Who was James Clerk Maxwell?
faq.answer: A British theoretical physicist and mathematician born in Edinburgh, Scotland, recognized as the founder of electromagnetism for establishing a unified foundation for electrical and magnetic phenomena.

faq.question: What is Maxwell's most important achievement?
faq.answer: Laying a unified foundation for electrical and magnetic phenomena and establishing electromagnetism, as well as presenting 'Maxwell's equations' that mathematically structured this.

faq.question: What contribution did Maxwell make to color photography?
faq.answer: In 1861, he created the first color photograph by applying the principle that all colors can be expressed by mixing the three primary colors.

faq.question: When and where was Maxwell born, and when did he die?
faq.answer: Born on June 13, 1831, in Edinburgh, Scotland, and died of stomach cancer in Cambridge on November 5, 1879, at the age of 48.

timeline.event: Birth of Moctezuma II
Succeeded his uncle Ahuitzotl to become the 9th Huey Tlatoani of the Aztec Empire
Ruled the Aztec Empire
Death
The last direct male descendant of Moctezuma, Seño de Moctezuma, died in New Orleans, USA, ending the male line of descent
A descendant of Moctezuma, the Count of Miravalle, demanded the Mexican government to reinstate the 'Moctezuma pension'

keyAchievements.title: Rule of the Aztec Empire
keyAchievements.description: Ruled as the 9th Huey Tlatoani of the Aztec Empire from 1502 to 1520.

keyAchievements.title: Territorial expansion
keyAchievements.description: Expanded the Aztec Empire's territory to its greatest extent during his reign, reaching present-day Honduras and Nicaragua.

keyAchievements.title: Attempts to strengthen centralization
keyAchievements.description: Attempted to strengthen centralization by abolishing the Jaguar warrior and Eagle warrior systems.

keyAchievements.title: Encounter with Spanish conquistador Hernan Cortes
keyAchievements.description: Attempted to bribe Hernan Cortes and lured him to the capital Tenochtitlan, but was taken prisoner.

faq.question: When did Moctezuma II rule the Aztec Empire?
faq.answer: Moctezuma II ruled as the 9th Huey Tlatoani of the Aztec Empire from 1502 to 1520.

faq.question: What significant events occurred during Moctezuma II's reign?
faq.answer: During his reign, Spain began its conquest of the Aztecs in earnest, and the empire's territory reached its maximum extent, stretching to present-day Honduras and Nicaragua.

faq.question: How did Moctezuma II die?
faq.answer: According to Spanish records, he died on June 29, 1520, three days after being injured by stones and arrows while attempting to address his people.

faq.question: Are there any direct male descendants of Moctezuma II?
faq.answer: The last direct male descendant of Moctezuma died in 1836, ending the male line, but there are living descendants today through his daughter Tecuichpoch.

timeline.event: Birth of Tran Hung Dao, an imperial prince and general of the Tran dynasty in Great Viet
When an envoy of the Mongol general Uriyangkhadai demanded safe passage through Vietnam, Tran Hung Dao was assigned the duty of border defense
When Kublai Khan's son Toghon invaded Vietnam with an army of 500,000, Nhan Tong appointed Tran Hung Dao as the Commander-in-Chief to lead the army
The army led by Tran Hung Dao fought against the Mongol forces, but the capital Thang Long was captured, and the retired emperor and Nhan Tong fled to Thanh Hoa
Tran Hung Dao compiled the 'Binh thu yeu luoc' (Summary of Military Tactics) and wrote the 'Hich tuong si' (Proclamation to the Officers) to boost the troops' morale
The army led by Tran Hung Dao recaptured Thang Long and forced Toghon's Mongol army to retreat
When the Yuan dynasty's army invaded again, Tran Hung Dao withdrew his troops from Thang Long and annihilated the Yuan fleet at the Bach Dang River (Battle of Bach Dang)
Tran Hung Dao died, leaving the last words: 'Unite the army like parents and children, treat the people generously, and use that strength to achieve great endeavors'

keyAchievements.title: Repelling Mongol Invasions
keyAchievements.description: Successfully repelled massive invasions by the Yuan dynasty twice, in 1285 and 1287, safeguarding the independence of Vietnam.

keyAchievements.title: Military Strategy and Morale Boosting
keyAchievements.description: Authored the 'Binh thu yeu luoc' and the 'Hich tuong si' to boost the morale of his soldiers and provide effective military strategies.

keyAchievements.title: Victory at the Battle of Bach Dang
keyAchievements.description: Achieved a decisive victory in the Battle of Bach Dang in 1287 by using the strategy of planting wooden stakes in the riverbed to annihilate the Yuan fleet.

faq.question: What is Tran Hung Dao's real name?
faq.answer: Tran Hung Dao's real name is Tran Quoc Tuan, and Hung Dao is a title bestowed upon him later to praise his achievements.

faq.question: What books did Tran Hung Dao write?
faq.answer: His works include 'Binh thu yeu luoc' (Summary of Military Tactics) and 'Van Kiep tong bi truyen thu' (Esoteric Manual of Van Kiep).

faq.question: What is a famous statement made by Tran Hung Dao?
faq.answer: During the Mongol invasion in 1285, he showed his will to fight by saying, 'If Your Majesty wishes to surrender, please cut off my head first'.
